data.title: Cosan's Deleveraging Story Still Lacks A Solid Foundation
data.description:
Revisiting the Bear Case

The last time I wrote about Cosan, I had summarized the bearish thesis as follows:

“The company's thesis has been based on high leverage, since high-interest expenses accumulate as a result of the very high-interest rate scenario in Brazil, and dividend generation has been on the verge of insufficiency. Thus, Cosan is forced to divest to strengthen its cash position, something I do not see as healthy or sustainable for a holding company.”

To make it clear, Cosan is a conglomerate of companies (Rumo (RUMOF), Compass, Moove, Radar, and Raízen) that basically lives off dividends and the appreciation of the equity of its holdings. The problem with the thesis is that Cosan has become an overly complex structure, mixing several cyclical and cash-intensive segments (sugar, ethanol, fuels), causing the market to apply a "conglomerate discount" to the thesis.

The Numbers Don’t Tell a Clean Story

The latest earnings print (4Q25 and FY25) highlighted some unresolved issues at Cosan in my view. Deleveraging improved, with a 3.3x pro-forma net debt-to-EBITDA ratio vs. 3.7x in 3Q25, with expanded net debt falling by 58% YoY despite the fact that dividends received also fell by 41% in the year, with an interest coverage of 0.9x—showing that Cosan still does not cover its interest expenses with its own cash flow.

The biggest problem, as I see it, is that this debt reduction did not come organically. Cosan carried out a capital increase of R$10.5 billion, diluting its shareholders, and sold core assets (stake in Vale (VALE)). So, this "improvement" in Cosan's reported results came much more from balance sheet engineering than via cash generation.

The structural risk for the holding company remains very high, as I view it, since with Cosan not fully controlling the flow, the dependence of Raízen, Rumo, and Compass on allocating their capital and eventually entering a bad cycle leaves Cosan "in the lurch", with dividends being reduced.

Turning to Raízen, this is the "elephant in the room". In early March, Raízen—which practically plants and processes sugarcane and transforms it into sugar and biofuel, but also distributes fuels (Shell in Brazil)—filed for out-of-court reorganization, with a debt of R$65 billion (the largest out-of-court reorganization case in Brazil).

About ~50% of creditors agreed to negotiate, but the real risk of this process for Cosan is if there is a conversion of debt into shares, where Cosan's stake may fall. If Raízen was previously seen as Cosan's main growth asset (energy transition), it is fair to say that today it is the weakest spot for the thesis. Naturally, this tends to lengthen the dividend "drought" and also creates a need for additional capital injections.

Finally, I would also bring as negative highlights Cosan’s drop in consolidated EBITDA by -15% in FY25. Only Raízen added with a -25% YoY drop in EBITDA. Radar and Compass fell -6% and -1% YoY, respectively, and only Rumo managed to grow 4%. This shows how Cosan's scenario does not come from a single factor, but from a spread deterioration, which is why deleveraging cannot be organic. At the same time, the market is getting skeptical about Cosan’s capital allocation strategy in the face of the bad timing of past acquisitions (such as the acquisition of a stake in Vale), and this creates a perception of reactive and non-creative management.

It does little to help that the interest rate in Brazil (Selic) is at 14.75%—recently cut by 25 bps—putting pressure on debt rates. As the table below depicts, Cosan's FY25 financial result was -R$2.87 billion, even if it improved from -R$5.16 billion in 2024. Derivatives have improved quite a bit, but less mark-to-market and financial distortions still do not indicate structural improvement. If, in fact, the Selic enters a cycle of cuts (although I do not believe in an interest rate scenario below 14% this year), this could be a catalyst for CSAN, given its sensitivity to interest rates. But I reinforce that even with lower interest rates, it does not solve the structural problem that still needs to circumvent execution risks and possibly divest assets.

The Discount Is Not as Large as It Seems

Arguably, the best way to look at Cosan's valuation, as a holding company, is through a sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) analysis of its holdings and compare to its current market value.

Raízen, Rumo, and Comgás (which is part of Compass) are companies traded on the Brazilian stock exchange (B3), so here we can use their market cap as a proxy.

At the time of writing this paragraph, Raízen (in which Cosan has a 39% stake) has a market cap of BRL 5.7 billion. Rumo (where Cosan has a 30% stake) has a market cap of BRL 30.29 billion. And Comgás (where Cosan has an 88% stake indirectly), trades at BRL 16.63 billion, but here I will use an approximate market value of ~BRL 14.63 billion for Compass.

Now, on the non-listed side, I will use BRL 5 billion equity value for Moove and BRL 5 billion equity value for Radar in a more optimistic scenario. This would result in a market value of ~R$35.9 billion for Cosan, a holding discount of ~42%.

But that's the raw number. Factoring Cosan's net debt of R$9.7 billion into the calculation, adjusted SOTP would be ~R$26.2 billion, a holding discount of ~21%. I would say that, although relevant, this is not a huge discount.

Historically, holding companies in LATAM (such as Itaúsa) tend to trade in the 15–30% discount range depending on complexity, and I believe that CSAN likely deserves to trade toward the higher end of that range. This, in my view, weakens the argument that Cosan is trading at a steep discount in the face of structural headwinds and suggests that it may not be a clear mispricing in the way I understand it.

Final Remarks

I think it is not ideal that Cosan has become so event-driven. But ironically, that’s also the main risk to the bearish view.

Speculation is already circulating about potential sales of Cosan’s stake in Rumo (with the company already confirming that it is evaluating strategic alternatives). There is also a concrete IPO plan already filed by Compass that could be a "cleaner" way to deleverage. I would say that the management team’s explicit statement in the earnings call that "we are considering every single business as a potential target to be sold partially" reinforces that selling its assets to deleverage is very much on the table. This may well be a healthier outcome to put Cosan in a position where it can deleverage organically in the foreseeable future and be able to cover its interest expenses with its own cash flow.

But other than that, I remain pessimistic about Cosan's thesis, and I would not dare to touch it as long as there is a clear path of organic and sustainable deleveraging. As I see the thesis at the moment, the turning point goes beyond simply reducing debt and instead lies in the holding company's ability to sustain its own cash flows—i.e., when dividends consistently cover financial expenses again. Until then, any improvement that is not structural but cosmetic and leaves the thesis at the mercy of events is likely to result in limited asymmetry. That being said, I reiterate my bearish call on CSAN.

data.title: Kanzhun: Focus On The Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Kanzhun Ltd. (BZ) previously with a buy rating, as I believe the investments in AI are truly helping BZ to improve recruiters' ROI and job seekers' engagement. Although the share price has gone the wrong way vs. what I expected, I am still giving this a buy rating as the core business is still growing at a healthy pace, white-collar hiring is recovering, and the customer base is broadening across smaller enterprises and lower-tier cities. More importantly, AI-related revenue has reached meaningful scale and is growing faster than the rest of the platform.

4Q25 earnings (announced on 18th March)

BZ fundamental performance continues to be solid. In the most recent quarter, revenue was up double digits as well, at 14% y/y, driving total revenue to RMB2.08 billion. Revenue from online recruitment services to enterprise customers was the main driver here, up 14.6% y/y, with healthy paid enterprise customer growth. Average monthly active users for the quarter were also up ~10% y/y, from 52.7 million to 58 million. Revenue growth came with lower operating costs, resulting in EBIT growing by ~80% y/y to RMB686 million. On an adjusted basis, that figure was lower at ~37% y/y, but still strong. Adj. net income, as a result, grew 25.4% y/y to RMB905.9 million.

White-collar hiring is recovering

BZ's stock price decline is indeed worrisome, and I can get why the market is reacting that way. The bear case narratives go something like this: If white-collar demand stayed weak, or if AI ended up reducing hiring activity, then BZ could have ended up as a low-growth platform with some margin help but not much else. Apparently, this is not the case, and there are data to back this up. Management said white-collar recruitment demand recovered steadily in 2025, and after adjusting for Chinese New Year timing, average daily job postings in the 15 days after the holiday surged by a double-digit percentage y/y. Management also said manufacturing, electronics and communications, semiconductors, automotive, advertising and media all grew faster, while newly added and active job postings for TMT sectors accelerated significantly. Overall, white-collar job postings grew positively y/y.

Previously, for BZ, the equity story was largely anchored against blue-collar hiring, but with white-collar hiring also back in the game, this significantly improves the narrative since there are two growth drivers here. Based on the comments given, BZ is effectively telling us the recovery is already showing up in job posting activity, and importantly, it is coming from a broad range of industries, which suggests the demand is more durable.

Customer base and ARPPU

The customer base also widened for the better. Total paid enterprise customers went up ~12% y/y to a total of 6.83 million, and management said revenue contribution from third-tier and lower-tier cities was 25% in Q4. They also said revenue from enterprises with <100 employees went >50% for the first time in 2025. This is a pretty significant development, in my view, as a broader mix across city tiers and smaller businesses means BZ demand has become more resilient (now that it leans on a wider base of demand, not just a handful of large accounts or one industry).

Normally, this kind of mix shift toward SMEs should pressure ARPPU, since smaller customers tend to spend less, but the fact that ARPPU stayed broadly stable despite this suggests BZ's value proposition is stronger and/or more customers are spending more. Either way, it's a good outcome and sets up well for further ARPPU gains as BZ ramps up monetization efforts (more on this below on AI).

AI is becoming a real revenue driver

BZ's AI efforts are shaping up well. Please refer to my previous writeup for some examples of BZ's AI solutions, but a brief recap is that the AI solutions are enabling BZ to not just help employers and job seekers find each other. It is moving closer to the hiring process itself, which matters for the monetization story because the monetization potential is much larger than simply selling/routing traffic. If AI can help employers hire faster and with better results, BZ has a stronger case to capture more of the recruitment budget. And we know for a fact that this is happening, as revenue from closed-loop services had already reached the scale of hundreds of millions of RMB by FY2025 (per the transcript), and this business was growing faster than the rest of the platform.

In my view, that is the clearest proof that AI is becoming a real revenue driver. Previously, the AI narrative was more of a theory and lacked tangible revenue numbers for investors to be confident. But now, management is explicitly saying this business is already meaningful in size and growing faster than the core platform. I'd say this is a real revenue driver, and the market should attach value to it.

A capital return plan helps the stock

Finally, I think attention should be spent on the capital return story too. BZ's board has approved a plan to allocate no less than 50% of the previous year's adj. net income to dividends and share repurchases over the three-year period starting from 2026. They have also increased the repurchase program limit to US$400 million (this is ~6% yield). This itself is not a game changer to buy the stock, but I think it does provide support to the stock (i.e., a floor). Add on the core business getting better and the AI efforts showing traction; this capital return angle does make the overall story look better.

Valuation

I recognize that BZ's valuation has fallen off the cliff, from 16x NTM PE when I wrote about it previously to the current ~10.6x. My view is that the market is paying attention to the wrong parts of the business. Specifically, some market participants might have extrapolated management's Q1 2026 guide of high-single-digit growth for the full year, and because of this, believed that BZ's growth profile has structurally worsened. Some of them might also be worried about the pressure on margins as BZ puts in more AI investments. To me, all these are short-term views that don't truly reflect BZ's long-term growth profile. What readers should focus on is the fact that white-collar demand has improved for BZ and that AI is now a real revenue driver.

Relative to the peers, BZ now trades at its lowest premium vs. the average, which I don't think is fair given the growth profile. Consensus expects BZ to grow revenue by ~30+% over the next 24 months, and that is the highest within the peer group. Even PageGroup (MPGPF) which is only expected to grow by ~4%, is trading at 15x. This valuation discount should close as BZ executes and grows as expected, and if we see a re-rate back to 16x, that is almost a 50% upside.

Risk

If hiring demand softens again after the post-CNY strength, it could really weigh on investors' confidence as they start to believe growth is going to slow for the rest of the year. The investments needed to ramp BZ's AI efforts also come at a cost, and depending on how fast BZ is able to ramp up monetization here, the step up in cost could eat into margins and weigh on earnings.

Conclusion

Despite the share price performance, I give BZ a buy rating. I think the market is too focused on near-term noise such as softer Q1 guided growth and the cost of AI investment, while missing the bigger story. White-collar hiring has improved, the core platform is still delivering solid growth, and AI is starting to show up as a real revenue driver. With the stock now trading at a much lower multiple than before, I believe the current setup still offers attractive upside if execution stays on track.

data.title: Darden: Decelerating Comps And Inflation Pressures
data.description:
So far in 2026, investors are focused on the macro story: a war in Iran, AI disruption, skyrocketing oil prices, and a shaky U.S. consumer. Very few companies, especially those in sectors exposed to consumer spending like the restaurant sector, have been able to escape the broad-based pessimism in the markets.

Darden Restaurants, Inc. (DRI), however, is one major outlier. The parent company behind Olive Garden and Longhorn Steakhouse has seen its share price rise modestly this year, defying weakness elsewhere in the sector (especially among fast-casual restaurants). Can Darden truly justify this outperformance?

I last wrote a “Sell” article on Darden in November, when the stock was trading around $180 per share. Since then, a number of factors have moved against the stock's favor even further. Share prices have increased (even while many restaurant peers have floundered), making its relative valuation less appealing. Same-restaurant sales growth has also compressed, calling into question Darden's enriched valuation multiples. At the same time, the company is also facing some pressure from commodity inflation, which makes us uncertain about its ability to generate meaningful earnings growth in the year ahead. With all of this in mind, I'm reiterating my “Sell” rating on Darden.

Let's touch briefly first on commodities inflation. For the near term, the company is expecting high single-digit inflation in beef, which represents 25% of Darden's food basket, and double-digit inflation in seafood, which is 9%. “Other” costs (27% of spending), which may include paper and packaging, are also expected to rise in the mid-single digits, while the company is expecting inflation in all other categories except dairy and oil, which are expected to reduce in price.

Yes, Darden has hedges in place, and it notes it is ~80% covered against price movements. And yet we note that the company's margins have been bleeding over the past few quarters despite the high degree of coverage. At the same time, the Darden brands that are doing the best in terms of same-restaurant sales are also showcasing the weaker segment margin profiles.

All of this leads up to the question: Can we justify Darden's valuation? The company has recently updated its fiscal FY26 outlook (the year that ends soon, in May 2026) to indicate 9.5% sales growth on 4.5% same-restaurant sales growth and 70 new openings (stronger than 7.5%-8.5% total growth, 2.5%-3.5% same-restaurant sales growth, and 65 openings previously), while slightly bumping up pro forma EPS expectations to $10.57-$10.67 (with $0.25 of expected benefit from a 53-week fiscal year this year).

For next year, FY27, Wall Street is expecting Darden to deliver $13.70 billion in revenue (+4% y/y revenue growth) and $11.38 in pro forma EPS, which is 7% earnings growth (or 10% excluding the benefit of the 53-week year in FY26).

This positions Darden's valuation multiples at:

  • 18.9x FY26 P/E.
  • 17.7x FY27 P/E.

In my view, it's difficult to justify a high-teens P/E multiple for a restaurant company with decelerating comp sales and potential margin challenges.

As shown above, Darden sits on the higher end of valuations in the restaurant sector, especially when compared to other full-service, sit-down chains like The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE), which is just trading at ~14x forward earnings.

Continue to steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 recap

Let's now go through Darden's latest fiscal Q3 (February quarter) results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Darden's revenue grew 6% y/y to $3.34 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.33 billion (+5% y/y) by just a one-point margin. What we are wary of, however, is the slight deceleration in same-restaurant sales growth. As a reminder, this metric measures the revenue growth from existing restaurants, without including the contribution of newly opened or recently closed stores.

As shown above, total Darden brand same-restaurant sales grew 4.2% y/y, which decelerated 50 bps versus 4.7% total-company same-restaurant sales in Q2. Interestingly, LongHorn Steakhouse and the Fine Dining segment (which includes higher-end seafood chains like Capital Grille and Eddy V's) saw an improvement to 7.2% and 2.1% same-restaurant sales growth in Q4, respectively, which was a 170 bps and 270 bps sequential lift. That said, this improvement was more than offset by the largest brand, Olive Garden, showcasing a 270 bps sequential decline to just 3.2% same-restaurant sales growth.

The good news here is that Darden still believes it's outperforming its industry, which management says is rather flat on same-restaurant sales. Per CEO Ricardo Cardenas' remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We've been consistently outperforming industry same-restaurant sales, and this quarter our gap widened as each of our 4 largest brands exceeded the industry by more than 400 basis points. All of our segments delivered positive same-restaurant sales as our restaurant teams continue to be brilliant with the basics, once again leading to impressive guest satisfaction scores [...]

Olive Garden delivered positive same-restaurant sales of 3.2% for the quarter, driven by strong operational execution even with 3 fewer weeks of price pointed promotions than last year. The restaurant teams are focused on ensuring every guest is offered a free refill on breadsticks and soup or salad. This led to a new all-time high guest satisfaction score for service and matched their all-time high for overall guest satisfaction. In January, Olive Garden completed the rollout of the lighter portion section of their menu, adding 7 more dishes under $15.

Here's the issue, however: the stronger-performing brands are those with the weaker margins. We note in particular that LongHorn Steakhouse, whose 7.2% comps growth carried Darden's quarter, fell to an 18.6% segment profit margin (-110 bps y/y) and has a 440 bps margin deficit to Olive Garden.

We can see the impact of higher beef prices eating into LongHorn's margin potential. The clear issue here: as Darden's sales mix tilts into these lower-margin businesses, overall company earnings growth will slow—even if same-restaurant sales growth is outmuscling peers.

As shown in the chart below, restaurant-level EBITDA (an industry measure that excludes corporate overhead spend) clocked in at $703.6 million, or a 21.0% margin that fell 30 bps y/y (with a -50 bps headwind from food and beverage inflation partially offset by favorable labor costs).

Again, we don't think necessarily that Darden is doing poorly, but its valuation multiples, which sit above other full-service chains like Cheesecake, already price the stock for perfection and put it at risk for decay.

Key takeaways

In my view, Darden's outperformance since the start of 2026 is a good chance to take gains off the table and invest in other names. A macro overhang sits ahead of us, especially as higher oil prices hit the pump: fewer consumers in the United States may be willing to go out, especially to higher-ticket restaurants like LongHorn Steakhouse. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: First Watch Might Still Be A Good Long-Term Investment
data.description:
Investors may still be underestimating First Watch (FWRG). My original buy call doesn’t look so good now. First Watch’s guidance was weaker than I expected. It looks like 2026 will be a tough year for restaurants. This modern diner might have to raise prices later this year even though it’s been trying to limit price hikes. But I think the long-term investment thesis for First Watch is still intact. This modern diner could still replace the old diners and eventually become a national diner chain. It’s still planning to build lots of new restaurants. I also think its current 2026 guidance is achievable. And based on that guidance, it looks undervalued right now.

First Watch ended the year with strong overall growth. It reported 20.2% revenue growth in Q4 2025. But it also reported -1.9% traffic. One of the reasons why I said First Watch was a buy last time was because its traffic rose in the third quarter, so that’s not a good sign. Maybe its strong Q3 results were an outlier after all. While First Watch still posted 3.1% same-store sales growth for the quarter, that’s because it raised its prices last year. Higher prices also helped First Watch strengthen its margins. The most important metric, restaurant-level operating margin, rose 20 basis points to 19% for the quarter.

Now, it looks like First Watch might also report negative traffic in 2026. It might not build as many new restaurants in 2026 either. While I think its high P/E could be misleading because of restaurant construction costs, it also has a forward P/E of 63. Investors might overlook First Watch’s high P/E ratio if it can keep growing rapidly. If its growth slows down, its bottom line will become more important. But in 2027, if the economy improves, First Watch could also post stronger comps and build a lot more restaurants. So, the first thing I’d consider is First Watch’s long-term growth potential.

First Watch Plans to Have 2,200 Restaurants Eventually

In its 2025 10-K, First Watch pointed out that it could eventually reach 2,200 locations. It had 633 restaurants at the end of 2025. Its long-term target is low double-digit location growth. If it can increase its location count by 10% per year, it would have 2,185 restaurants in 13 years. So, this could become a major nationwide restaurant chain in 2038. Right now, it’s still a regional one. First Watch currently has locations in 23 states. And I think it’s valid for First Watch’s stock to have a growth premium because of this long-term plan.

But First Watch also plans to expand at a slightly slower pace in 2026. If you use the midpoint of its guidance and subtract the 3 locations it’s closing, it’s planning to add 58 net new restaurants in 2026. That’s a 9% growth rate. It makes sense for First Watch to slow down its growth because of economic weakness right now. I think the restaurant could probably speed up its expansion pace again once the economy improves.

First Watch Is Also Buying Franchised Restaurants

Some of the other restaurants that I’m following right now, such as Papa John’s (PZZA), are refranchising company-owned restaurants to improve their margins. So, it’s interesting that First Watch is making the opposite move. It’s buying restaurants from its franchisees and plans to keep doing that. Company-owned restaurants often perform better because the company has direct control over them, but they also have lower margins because the company has to pay all of the operating expenses. It also sounds to me like Wall Street analysts may not like this strategy because they usually prefer asset-light companies.

First Watch bought 19 restaurants from its franchisees in 2025 and has the right to buy back another 12 restaurants in 2026. So, the company can add incremental revenue by buying back more restaurants in 2026. I’d estimate that First Watch might be able to raise its revenue by 1 percentage point in 2026 by buying back locations from franchise owners. And First Watch can also use this lever to maintain its revenue growth in future years when it gains the rights to buy back more restaurants. But it still expects its revenue growth to slow down a lot this year.

First Watch’s Guidance for 2026 Is Weaker Than Expected

In my previous article, I said I expected First Watch to post 20% revenue growth in 2026. Now, the company is guiding for 12% to 14% revenue growth instead. This explains why its stock dropped after its Q4 report, but I think its new guidance is still achievable. First Watch is going to get a 9% uplift from new locations, and it can get a 1% uplift from buying back restaurants. And it’s also guiding for 1% to 3% comps. First Watch could achieve that goal by raising its prices, although it might report negative full-year traffic as a result.

But one of the reasons why I said First Watch was a buy in my previous article was that its traffic was rising, and now it sounds like that might not happen in 2026. First Watch is still being cautious about price increases, but it might raise prices later in the year. Higher commodity costs might not be transitory after all. Of course, this also means that other diners will have to raise their menu prices because their ingredient costs went up. First Watch should still have more pricing power than other diners because it attracts wealthy consumers who want healthier food. But lower-income consumers might go to the grocery store instead.

First Watch still expects to perform better than other restaurants in 2026. On the call, First Watch CEO Christopher Tomasso said, “And then on the Black Box, to be more specific, in my commentary, I talked about their current projection for the year is roughly a 3% industry-wide same-restaurant traffic decline.” Of course, if restaurants all report lower traffic and higher expenses in 2026, they might all trade at lower multiples.

The Main Risk Factor Is First Watch’s Premium Business Model

The restaurant sector is highly promotional right now. Pizzerias are using deep discounts to eliminate their competitors. Like First Watch, fast-casual pizzerias also use better ingredients than their peers and charge higher prices. And like I’ve said in my other articles, these pizzerias are shutting down because they’re losing business to competitors with lower prices. The thesis for First Watch depends on wealthier customers paying up for better food. But some of its customers might still decide to trade down. And other customers might buy meals from grocery stores.

Updated Comparative Valuation for First Watch

Like I said before, investors are paying more attention to the bottom line now. But I still think investors are valuing this restaurant incorrectly. If you look at its P/S ratio, it’s trading at a huge discount. First Watch currently has a forward P/S ratio of 0.54. That’s far lower than other growth restaurants’ P/S multiples. On March 23, Cava (CAVA) had a forward P/S of 6.80. And Dutch Bros (BROS) had a forward P/S of 3.11. Darden (DRI) had a forward P/S of 1.77. And Sweetgreen (SG), which is trading at a discount to all of these chains, still had a higher forward P/S than First Watch at 0.88.

[object HTMLElement]

Updated First Watch Valuation for 2026

It sounds like First Watch is considering a price increase in the second half of 2026 now. So, with the benefit of higher prices, it could still post 3% full-year comps in 2026. And I also think it will get a 1% revenue boost from buying back restaurants. And, again, it’s going to increase its location count by 9%. That adds up to 13% revenue growth. And that’s also the midpoint of First Watch’s 2026 revenue growth guidance. So, I’d project that First Watch’s revenue will rise 13% from $1.223 billion to $1.382 billion.

First Watch also reported full-year adjusted EBITDA of $120.9 million in 2025, so its adjusted EBITDA margin was 9.9%. If its EBITDA margin stays the same, it would report $136.8 million in 2026. That’s near the middle of its guidance range, which is $132 to $140 million. It sounds like First Watch expects its margins to remain about the same in 2026. It might have to raise its prices in the second half of the year to do that, but that’s still doable. So, I’ll estimate First Watch’s 2026 earnings under the assumption that it will report $136.8 million in adjusted EBITDA.

First Watch reported $75 million in depreciation in 2025, up from $57.7 million in 2024. That’s an increase of $17.3 million. First Watch is still building restaurants, although its pace has slowed down a bit. So, I’ll be conservative and project that its depreciation expense will rise another $17.3 million to $92.3 million in 2026.

The company also paid $16.7 million in interest, up from $12.6 million in 2024. It’s taking out loans to build restaurants and will need to borrow more money in 2026. It only has $21.2 million in cash right now. Again, I’ll be conservative and project that its interest costs will rise another $4.1 million to $20.8 million. After subtracting $113.1 million in depreciation and interest costs from $136.8 million in adjusted EBITDA, I get $23.7 million in EBIT. First Watch paid a 37% tax rate last year. So, if its tax rate is the same in 2027, its income would be $14.9 million. With 62.8 million shares outstanding, that’s full-year 2026 EPS of $0.24.

With a 2026 P/E of 63, my target price would be $15.12. This company’s current price is $12.35 per share. My updated price target is 22% higher than its current price. So, I’d say this stock is still a buy. And I’m using conservative assumptions. First Watch’s depreciation and interest costs might not rise as much this year as they did last year because the company is not planning to add as many new restaurants. Wall Street analysts currently have a price target of $19.50 and a strong buy rating on First Watch’s stock, so they’re even more confident in this company.

Conclusion

First Watch expects to grow at a slower rate in 2026. Conditions don’t look great for the restaurant sector right now. But I still think First Watch’s current guidance for 2026 is achievable. And I think that First Watch should also trade at a high P/E multiple because of its long-term growth potential. Its growth could accelerate in 2027 as the economy recovers. And if I estimate its 2026 results based on this guidance and its current multiples, this stock looks undervalued. So, I think First Watch is still a buy.

data.title: BGIN Blockchain: From Altcoin Weakness To Bitcoin Opportunity
data.description:
Last time I spoke about Bgin Blockchain Limited (BGIN), despite the Strong Sell outlook, mostly due to the bearish crypto market and the company's elevated exposure to altcoins, I concluded by saying that BGIN could be a really good stock to hold in a future crypto bull market due to its, at the time, intent to manufacture a Bitcoin mining ASIC. Since the last article, the stock price fell 25.6% compared to a flat S&P 500 change; Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell about 25%, and Kaspa (KAS-USD) and ALEO, which are altcoins more related to the company, fell about 13% and 60%, respectively. While I am still strongly bearish on cryptos in general, the recent press release is something that could change the company's near-term outlook. In the last month, BGIN has experienced some positive momentum, which was certainly due to the MAR 16 press release, where the company announced a successful tape-out of a new 4-nanometer (nm) Bitcoin mining chip called BT1, which is an important shift in the company's strategy, directing it towards a more resilient market, and in my view, if they have success with this new mining chip, the stock could see some positive momentum, even in this bearish crypto market.

BGIN designs and manufactures application-specific integrated circuits (ASIC). The company focused on manufacturing ASICs for altcoins like Kaspa, Aleo, and Alephium, and they are known for the ICERIVER ASICs, which have very good power consumption to performance ratios. The ICEREVERs were made with 8nm and 12nm chips, and as said before, the new Bitcoin ASIC will have a 4nm chip, which is an important technology jump for the company, but it is still lagging in comparison with top competitors, which are already seeking 3nm and 2nm for their newest ASIC models. Despite the comparison, BGIN still can achieve a solid efficiency, with a lower joules per terahash ratio (J/TH), which is one of the most important metrics for Bitcoin mining ASICs, given the current electricity demand increase of AI data centers pushing energy prices higher. The BT1 project is already past the initial stage, which is usually riskier and more expensive, and it is now in the testing phase. If the company successfully delivers a competitive ASIC, it could put this new model alongside the most cost-effective options in the market. Another positive is that BGIN chairman Oisin Li and its independent director, Paul Tsang, had purchased additional Class A ordinary shares of the Company in January, during the tape-out phase, in January 2026, which shows their commitment to this new step the company is taking.

If it wasn't for this announcement, I would still hold on to my expectation of seeing falling Bitcoin prices pushing altcoins even lower until near the end of Q3 2026, which was usually the low of the cycle, and expect BGIN to also continue declining. But if their new ASICs deliver a strong performance, around 12-14 J/TH range. That would draw significant attention, and the stock could trade at higher multiples. Additionally, given that Bitcoin is more stable than altcoins, BGIN's operation could become profitable again, like in 2023 and 2024, when the company achieved about 54% and 21% net margins, respectively, and even if Bitcoin prices decline more, let's say, to $45,000, ASIC manufacturers continue to be profitable. Their break-even point would depend on many factors. In general, the lower the J/TH, the more efficiently the company can operate, allowing it to remain profitable even at lower Bitcoin prices.

Model Manufacturer Efficiency (J/TH) Chip Technology Cooling
BGIN BT1 BGIN Blockchain TBA 4nm TBA
Avalon A16XP Canaan 12.8 5nm Air
Avalon A16 Canaan 13.8 5nm Air
Antminer S21 Pro Bitmain 15 5nm Air
WhatsMiner M60S++ MicroBT 15.9 5nm Air
Hashivo B1 Hashivo 11 4nm Hydro

On the downside, the Bitcoin ASIC market is very competitive, with the likes of Bitmain and MicroBT, which are the companies partnering with the largest Bitcoin farms, with bigger teams and deeper pockets. To compete, BGIN must reveal a really good product with a reasonable price. BGIN seems to be moving in the right direction, mentioning "rigorous cost control" in the press release. But if they fail to do so, their cash position on the balance sheet is really small, at $23.9 million as of JUN 2025, while cash from operations averages about negative $50 million every 6 months, and on the income statement, earnings from continuous operations are also negative, at $30.2 million. Meaning that the company might run out of cash and may have to dilute shareholders or incur debt to continue operations.

Another risk is the inventory, which was pretty high at JUN 2025, valued at $23.4 million, during peak optimism in the crypto market, and we can just hope they have sold their inventory instead of fully replenishing it before this crypto bear market. We also don't know when the company will finish testing, release important data, and start production. It may take 3-6 months, and in the meantime, the altcoin market is melting, and demand for BGIN's altcoin ASICs is extremely low. We also may not be at the market bottom; even if the new ASICs are competitive, crypto prices may decline further, putting more downward pressure on the stock.

To conclude, BGIN has no room for error. The company is already low in cash, while altcoin prices are really low and may continue declining. Bitcoin prices may also continue declining, but if the next Bitcoin ASIC, BT1, achieves efficiency in the 12-14 J/TH range, that would place it within the competitive range of current-generation ASICs, improving the company's financial prospects. There is a chance the stock will react positively, even with a bearish crypto outlook. Conversely, if their new ASIC isn't competitive enough, above 16 J/TH, it would likely limit adoption and weaken the investment case, and it could accentuate the stock decline. Given the upside potential, instead of a strong sell, I now view the stock as a "Hold".

data.title: Piedmont Realty Trust: Deeply Discounted, Significant Upside Potential
data.description:
Office-focused real estate investment trust (“REIT”), Piedmont Realty Trust, Inc. (PDM), is one office operator that I continue to remain bullish on despite the continuing slump in the stock value.

The stock has lost over 60% of its value over the last five years. PDM also did away with its quarterly dividend payout, leaving investors with very little to grasp onto apart from patience for an eventual rebound.

I have provided continuously bullish coverage on PDM in the past, and though the stock hasn’t performed to my expectations, I still believe long-term investors will be rewarded with sizeable upside. While that timetable for recovery is uncertain, PDM’s operating results suggest the recovery is overdue, in my view.

PDM Stock Key Metrics

In its most recent investor presentation in March 2026, PDM provided a core funds from operations (“FFO”) range of $1.47–$1.53/share. At the midpoint, current share pricing would indicate a 4.4x forward multiple. In my view, this is incredibly cheap for both the sector and the overall real estate market.

Shares grade as an “A” on valuation by the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants, and for good reason. Office-based peers such as SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) and Vornado Realty Trust (VNO), for example, trade at multiples of 8.5x and 11x, respectively. PDM trades at just a fraction of these NYC-based peers.

The analyst communities are similarly bullish, with those on Wall Street especially so. The caveat, however, is that the stock is lightly covered by both the SA and Wall Street communities.

Nonetheless, it’s still worth noting that consensus targets see about 45% upside potential in the stock. That’s eye-catching for sure. But what is, perhaps, even more surprising is that even if PDM reaches this potential, it would still be valued at just 6.5x its forward FFO. That would still be a reasonable valuation, in my view.

PDM Operating Performance

PDM just wrapped up what I would consider a standout leasing year, and I believe the results speak for themselves.

For the full year 2025, PDM leased 2.5M square feet, representing roughly 16% of its portfolio. This was the highest level of activity in over a decade and well ahead of original expectations. I believe that kind of volume is hard to ignore, especially in a sector that many still view as challenged.

Drilling into Q4, leasing activity remained robust with nearly 700K square feet signed across 60 transactions. Furthermore, a significant portion of this was driven by new tenants, and this signals to me real demand rather than just renewals.

Even more encouraging, expansions have outpaced contractions for six straight quarters, pointing to tenants actually growing into space again.

Pricing has also been a clear bright spot. Cash and accrual rental rates increased 12% and 21%, respectively, during the quarter. This continued a multi-quarter trend of strong rent roll-ups.

Meanwhile, occupancy ticked higher to just under 90%. Better yet, there’s still a sizeable backlog of nearly 2M square feet of signed leases that have yet to commence.

What Is The Outlook For PDM Stock?

While it may sound cliché, I believe PDM may be at an inflection point.

Within the Q4 print, it seemed to me that occupancy likely bottomed in Q4 2025. And from here, the story is likely to shift from stabilization to growth.

In terms of the guidance itself, PDM is guiding to a midpoint of $1.50/share in core FFO. This is solid YOY growth. And what’s driving that? It’s a signed business.

PDM has nearly 2M square feet of leases that haven’t commenced yet, the bulk of which will come online throughout 2026. That alone should provide a noticeable lift to both occupancy and earnings.

In fact, the management team expects occupancy rates to jump roughly 400 basis points this year. That’s a big move, in my view, and it should translate into mid-single-digit same-store NOI growth.

Moreover, lower interest expense from refinancing activity provides an additional tailwind.

Leasing demand also appears to remain healthy, with expectations for another 1.7 to 2 million square feet of activity in 2026. When you combine that with the limited new supply across the office market, the outlook looks increasingly favorable.

Is PDM Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At this point, I understand the frustration with PDM. A 60%+ decline over five years and the absence of a dividend is not an easy combination to stomach, especially in a REIT where income is typically a key part of the story.

That said, I believe there’s been a clear disconnect between what the company is doing operationally and how the market is valuing the stock, and I think the current setup still favors long-term investors.

The valuation alone is difficult to ignore, trading at just over 4x forward FFO, particularly when peers command meaningfully higher multiples. More importantly, the leasing momentum is real, as seen in PDM’s most recent results; pricing power is improving, and a significant portion of future growth is already under contract but not yet reflected in earnings.

Of course, risks remain. The office sector still carries negative sentiment, and PDM’s lack of a dividend removes a key layer of investor support in the near term. Timing also remains uncertain, which has clearly tested investor patience.

Even with the risks, I still believe the potential rewards for PDM still outweigh the risks at current levels. For investors willing to take a longer-term view, I continue to see PDM as a buy.

data.title: CHAT: Global AI Growth At A Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Overview

These are exciting times in the market with the growth of artificial intelligence. It seems like businesses across every sector are trying to utilize AI in some capacity to increase earnings. It can be difficult to assess hundreds of companies and determine which opportunities are the highest quality. The Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) makes it extremely easy by providing direct exposure to many of the large-cap leaders across the technology sector. The fund is still relatively new, with an inception dating back to May of 2023. Despite the short operating history, CHAT has delivered excellent performance and demonstrated its ability to participate in the upside momentum of the technology market.

Looking at the performance on a YTD basis, we can see that CHAT has significantly outperformed traditional index-tracking ETFs, such as the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). CHAT's outperformance during this time of uncertainty may be attributed to the slight global exposure that the fund provides, as well as the exposure to the actual builders of the AI infrastructure. CHAT tends to focus its holdings on the large-cap players across the technology sector while minimizing exposure to more speculative bets.

The fund also offers a higher-than-average dividend yield of 2.6%, which may be attractive for investors that prefer to collect a dividend from their holdings. While I am optimistic about the outlook of the AI sector, there are some challenges that can slow growth momentum. For instance, massive capex spending levels are causing investors to question whether or not this will lead to higher earnings. Furthermore, CHAT's concentration on the tech sector means that investors should anticipate that there's the potential for greater volatility compared to standard index-tracking ETFs.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund overview, CHAT has total assets under management of $1.05B that are spread across 43 different positions. The fund is actively managed, so the expense ratio is a bit high at 0.75% of assets. So an investment into CHAT means that we are trusting management's selection process across the AI sector. After reading through the fund's prospectus, CHAT prioritizes three sub-sectors within the AI market: platform developers, infrastructure hardware, and software. By allocating its positions accordingly, CHAT can directly align shareholders to participate in the expansion of the AI sector.

Expanding on this focus, we can see that CHAT also provides exposure to some global regions. U.S.-focused investments still make up 66.51% of assets, so I wouldn't consider this a dedicated global fund. Instead, CHAT offers a way for investors to get a sprinkle of global exposure while still participating in the growth of the U.S. leaders. CHAT's global exposure is primarily spread across different regions of Asia and Europe.

While CHAT takes a very refined approach with its holdings, this is guided by a very specific selection process. The management team uses natural language processing to analyze company filings, earnings calls, and other business data to come up with a 'GenAI' score for each of its holdings. This helps to ensure that CHAT maintains exposure to companies that are actually utilizing AI technology instead of using buzzwords to get attention without much actual substance behind their business model.

When looking at the top holdings of CHAT, we can see that investors are able to get direct exposure to some of the highest-quality businesses in the world. For instance, Alphabet (GOOG) is the largest position at 6.92% of assets. This is followed by many other notable businesses, including NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), just to name a few. So instead of trying to select a bunch of individual stocks, CHAT makes the entire process easy for long-term investors.

Valuation: Outlook Is Attractive

What I find interesting about this opportunity is that many of the top holdings within CHAT trade at a similar forward price-to-earnings ratio compared to the S&P 500! Many holdings trade at a lower forward price-to-earnings ratio than the Nasdaq-100 Index as well. This reinforces that the market sentiment around technology has really fallen, and opportunistic investors may be able to capitalize. For reference, the S&P 500 Index now trades at an approximate forward price-to-earnings ratio of 21.21x. Meanwhile, the Nasdaq-100 trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 21.74x, which is lower than the forward price-to-earnings ratio of ~26.52x at the start of 2025. To help visualize how attractively valued some of the positions are, here are the price-to-earnings ratios for CHAT's top holdings:

Forward Price-to-Earnings

  • Nvidia: 21.58x
  • Microsoft: 22.26x
  • Micron Technology (MU): 6.97x
  • Meta Platforms (META): 20.41x

To be fair, there are also many holdings within CHAT that trade at elevated valuations, but the point was to demonstrate that sentiment has temporarily suppressed many high-quality companies. Looking forward, I am fairly optimistic about the positive impact that AI will have across the technology sector. While the focus right now is on LLMs and different software, I believe that AI will help companies efficiently cut costs and improve operating margins over time. However, this is still speculative, and it is too early to definitively confirm this will be the case, especially while the large-cap leaders allocate massive amounts of capital towards capex spending.

Despite the challenges, data suggests that the size of the AI market will continue to grow rapidly over the next few years. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market will be valued at around $3.5T by 2033. Based on the current 2026 estimated value of $539.5B, this indicates a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% going forward. CHAT is directly aligned to capture the positive growth of the market with its holdings.

Another thing that I like about CHAT is that it primarily focuses on the large-cap holdings within the technology sector. These tend to be companies with excess levels of cash flow that can continue to navigate the challenges of the sector. By focusing on these large-cap players, the fund's exposure to smaller-cap speculative companies is quite limited, which is the kind of exposure that I prefer.

CHAT's structure and diversity are also appealing through the current market uncertainty. On a YTD basis, the Mag 7 have all pulled back from their all-time highs. However, CHAT has been able to outpace all the Mag 7 due to exposure to some non-U.S. companies that have held up very well, such as ASML Holding (ASML).

Risks and Peers

The irony of the AI market is that progress has moved very quickly. Several quarters ago, companies instantly experienced some positive momentum if there was any mention of AI on the earnings call. AI has become so efficient that now the market is nervous that the affordability and utility of AI tools will eliminate the moat for many software companies. For instance, Adobe (ADBE) has now declined by more than 39% over the last twelve months as sentiment shifted. There's no telling how much longer this negative momentum can continue for software companies, which may continue to suppress the share price for many of CHAT's holdings.

Another risk is the massive capex spending levels for many of the large-cap leaders. While these businesses are technically capable of supporting this elevated level of capex spending with their own cash flows, this created a high-pressure environment where investors are looking for a clear path to profitability in the future. If these businesses are unable to move beyond productivity and into the phase of a high-margin revenue stream, this may cause a re-rating of the entire sector. Just for reference, here are the estimated capex spending levels for the leaders:

  1. Amazon: ~$200B
  2. Alphabet: ~$175B-$185B
  3. Microsoft: ~$140B
  4. Meta: ~$115B-$135B

While the introduction of global securities helps with diversity, this introduces the vulnerability to foreign issues. For instance, CHAT may be negatively impacted by the continued health of the semiconductor supply chain or specific regulatory risks across the Asia markets through exposure to companies like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM).

Although CHAT's higher expense ratio has been justified over the last few quarters, the fund's short operating history still makes me question the cost-efficiency over a longer holding period. For instance, QQQ's expense ratio is much more reasonable at 0.18%. CHAT has outperformed QQQ since inception, but the outperformance was only relevant once the U.S. markets started to pull back from their highs. Prior to this, CHAT and QQQ's total return was nearly on par with each other between 2023 and the end of 2024.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I do believe that CHAT is an efficient long-term hold. While many of its positions trade at reasonable forward price-to-earnings ratios, I think that CHAT is a buy here. The fund's exposure to global companies has clearly demonstrated the potential to lead to outperformance during turbulent times in the U.S. markets. The fund is actively managed so the holdings can be shifted based on market conditions and challenges. The AI market is expected to experience rapid growth over the next few years, and CHAT directly positions investors to participate in this growth.

data.title: Crescent Energy: The Quiet Shale Powerhouse The Market Misses
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

The U.S. shale industry is experiencing a silent transition.

Crescent Energy (CRGY) lies in the heart of this change. Crescent is an independent oil and gas company that operates producing assets in major U.S. shale basins.

Basically, it is bringing together quality assets, streamlining operations and broadening its platform. However, the market continues to underestimate what the company has established.

Indeed, the previous year was not typical. Crescent had a superb Q4, which showed that its strategy is working. It continued to grow steadily in production and in cash flow.

More importantly, it's not necessarily about volume. The company is making acquisitions a repeatable platform, which can generate long-term value.

Moreover, it mostly carries liquids-intensive business, which means that increased oil prices are immediately converted into larger margins and cash flows. Meanwhile, the fact that the company is entirely U.S.-based insulates it against foreign operational risk.

Naturally, there are risks, but the overall story is simple. Crescent is performing and developing value that the market does not yet appreciate. The opportunity is real and based on fundamentals as opposed to speculation.

All in all, I would rate CRGY as a buy at current levels.

Geopolitical Oil Market Context

World attention has been attracted to tensions in the Middle East.

Nevertheless, to U.S. producers, oil prices are the central effect as opposed to direct operational risk. Fears surrounding the Strait of Hormuz have placed a premium on crude. Consequently, the world benchmark, Brent crude, and the primary U.S. benchmark, West Texas Intermediate, are trading at higher levels. Concisely, markets are putting a premium on uncertainty, and not a complete supply shock.

Well, this is important to Crescent. The company is completely working within the U.S. and thus it is not directly exposed to geopolitical conflict. Meanwhile, its liquids-heavy production has benefited from high oil prices.

Investors are not required to speculate on the fate of the conflict in Crescent’s case. The combination of operations, scale, and cost efficiency enables the company to translate increased prices of commodities into a rise in earnings and free cash flow.

This macro background supports the main thesis. The stock is underpriced since the market has not realized the benefits of the platform in capturing both the operational performance and the positive market environment.

Results Show Crescent Already Operating at a Higher Scale

The FY 2025 earnings ensure that it was not just an ordinary year. Production rose steadily and cash flow was solid throughout the year. Adjusted EBITDAX was more than $2 billion with free cash flow remaining robust even in a heavy acquisition phase.

It is obvious that the company is performing effectively. It is hard to sustain such performance, yet Crescent is proving both discipline and ambition. This provides a basis for future acquisitions and returns to shareholders.

In addition, Crescent invested approximately $5 billion of non-core assets in higher-rate positions in Eagle Ford, Permian and Uinta. The acquisition of Permian alone doubled the anticipated synergies. These actions show that the company is developing a platform that is capable of long-term growth, and operational efficiency will create value as time goes by. It is a matter of quality and scale rather than volume.

The Q4 results back this evaluation. Eagle Ford provided 174 Mboe per day, Permian contributed 28 Mboe per day despite 17 days of contribution by the acquisition, whereas Uinta contributed 23 Mboe per day. The rise in FCF was already a product of operational improvements, including more efficient completions and increased capital intensity.

These numbers are a sign that Crescent is pursuing its consolidation strategy successfully. The company is improving its assets whilst adding new ones, which is what a growing consolidator should do.

Crescent is already behaving as an integrated, scaled shale player. Its performance is a testament to an efficient growth strategy.

Crescent Turns Acquisitions into Real Value

Crescent is transforming acquisitions into a scalable, high-value platform that the market has not quite acknowledged. It uses three levers in its approach. These are

  1. Integration
  2. Operational efficiency and
  3. Diversification.

Each of these has a tangible value and provides Crescent with long-term optionality. In my opinion, this is the core of its upside.

Let's start with integration. The Permian acquisition has already provided $40 million of operational synergies, and management doubled its synergy goal. These benefits are attributed to efficient pad layouts, high laterals, enhanced artificial lift, and improvement of the supply chain. Interestingly, these synergies are measurable and replicable. Crescent is transforming acquisitions into actual operating performance. There are not many who are able to maintain this discipline.

The second lever is operational efficiency. In Eagle Ford, Permian, and Uinta, the drilling and completion expense dropped by 15% annually. The efficiency of completing has also become much better, about 35% more effective since 2023. These enhancements derive higher value out of every asset, increase returns on capital, and lay the groundwork for subsequent consolidation. This is what makes acquisitions a growth lever and not a short-term growth booster.

The last pillar is diversification. Crescent has diversified in minerals and royalties with overlapping positions to create accretive cash flows with low risk. This layer is resilient structurally. It enables the business to add value over time, and it finds a way of going around commodity cycles.

These levers are still underestimated in the market. Investors tend to juxtapose Crescent with past peers as opposed to its maturing platform. The stock is valued as a small-scale independent, but the company is already being a scaled consolidator. This gap is critical to the realization of why the operational playbook of Crescent can catalyze a significant re-rating.

Valuation Discussion

The valuation represents a market that is beginning to see some progress but is not quite convinced.

Consensus forward P/E for CRGY is estimated to be 9.1x and 8.9x for FY26 and FY27, respectively, and its forward P/S ratio is expected to be lower than 1.

This seems credible at a glance for an E&P company, but it lacks a major transition. Crescent is enhancing its margins by means of integration and cost management as well as quality of assets.

Notably, the market seems to be valuing the present performance, rather than performance stability. This is observable in the broad range of estimates, and it remains unclear whether Crescent can repeat its acquisition model.

Consequently, the stock has been trading at a discounted multiple even when there is good execution.

Meanwhile, the price action indicates that acceptance is accumulating. The above snapshot shows that CRGY stock currently trades between 30% and 35% above its key moving averages. This is a sign of better sentiment and reflects that there is some positive change already.

In the future, additional returns will probably depend more on earnings delivery than on multiple expansion alone.

Under a base case with steady execution, it is rational to assume a slight re-rating toward a 10-11x earnings. A 10.5x multiple on an estimated $1.38 FY26 EPS yields a value of around $14.5. This situation does not involve aggressive suppositions.

The upside is more significant in a bull case. As long as Crescent continues to provide synergies, keep its costs under control, and scale its platform, the market may attribute a 12-13x multiple premium. This would translate to a range between $17 and $18 or higher when applied to the 2027 estimated EPS of $1.42.

Moreover, earnings can surprise positively in case efficiency gains are sustained.

On the whole, Crescent is performing well, but the market has not fully recognized the strength and scalability of its model. This disparity favors a moderate yet evidently optimistic outlook.

Risks and Takeaway

One should keep in mind the risks.

For starters, it is impossible to disregard integration risk. The company has already made several acquisitions to create scale. Although the outcomes are impressive so far, there is a chance that the process of merging the operations in various basins may be even more time-consuming than expected. Anticipated re-rating could be slowed by any delays in the capture of synergies or the optimization of costs.

Then there is the issue of commodity price volatility. Liquids-heavy production is an advantage to Crescent, and a rise in oil prices increases the cash flow and margins. Oil markets are, however, uncertain. The continued decline in prices would lower the free cash flow and might restrain the companies' ability to expand. The investors should also understand that the stock is still affected by macro factors that are outside the management’s control.

Additionally, there is an execution and operational risk. Consistency is necessary to expand a platform across Eagle Ford, Permian, and Uinta. There may be some unforeseen operational difficulties or an increase in costs, which may affect performance. Although the management has been disciplined, investors must consider the aspect of handling numerous assets and the incorporation of new acquisitions.

Last but not least, there is still the practical risk of market perception lag. Despite positive performances, the stock might not re-rate at once since the market is not always quick to value structural adjustments.

These risks make the takeaway obvious. Crescent is implementing a strategic and scalable consolidation strategy. The stock has potential upside as the market adjusts to the reality of the company's transformation.

It is important to be patient and disciplined, although the structural narrative is solid and not wholly appreciated.

data.title: Geopolitical Volatility Driving Rapid Shifts In 2026 Oil & Gas Forecasts
data.description:
Analysts have lifted 2026 expectations for the world’s largest integrated oil and gas companies in recent weeks, as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East inject fresh volatility into energy markets and reshape near-term earnings prospects.

Crude prices have rallied sharply amid escalating tensions involving the Middle East, with markets pricing in a rising risk of supply disruption from a region that underpins a significant share of global oil flows. The prospect of interruptions to key transit routes, particularly the Strait of Hormuz, has pushed a geopolitical risk premium back into prices, reversing some of the weakness seen earlier this year.

This shift is clearly reflected in analyst forecasts. Consensus assumptions for 2026 crude oil and natural gas liquids prices have been revised higher compared with early March estimates, as stronger near-term price realizations feed into outlooks. However, the more notable change has been in profitability, where upgrades have been more pronounced.

Across the sector, analysts have raised forecasts for operating income, supported by stronger downstream performance and improved margin expectations. Refining and marketing businesses are expected to benefit from higher product prices and widening crack spreads, as refined fuel markets tighten more quickly than crude supply.

Upstream earnings expectations have also moved higher, reflecting improved near-term realizations and firmer pricing assumptions. The combination of stronger upstream pricing and resilient downstream margins is driving a broad-based uplift in profitability forecasts across the majors.

Notably, these upgrades are not being driven by volume growth. Production and refining capacity assumptions have remained broadly stable, and in some cases edged lower, indicating that the improved outlook is largely margin-driven rather than a function of higher output.

Looking ahead, the market will continue to be shaped by both geopolitical developments and underlying supply-demand dynamics. As seen in the recent analyst revisions, it is worth noting that consensus expectations are changing rapidly in response to evolving price signals and regional risks.

This article was published by Visible Alpha, part of S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Vs. Eli Lilly: Cheap Vs. Expensive, But No Clear Opportunity
data.description:
The GLP-1 market remains one of the most attractive growth opportunities in global pharma. However, I think the current market narrative has become overly simplified: Novo Nordisk (NVO) is increasingly seen as losing ground, while Eli Lilly (LLY) is viewed as the clear leader in the GLP-1 market.

In my view, the market is leaning too far in both directions.

Novo Nordisk appears cheap but faces headwinds that are unlikely to disappear any time soon. Eli Lilly continues to execute very well, but a lot is already priced in. What's more, the GLP-1 market is transitioning from a shortage-driven phase into a more normalized and more competitive environment.

As a result, I see limited risk-reward for both companies at current levels and take a "Hold" stance on both companies.

GLP-1 Market

The GLP-1 obesity and diabetes market has grown significantly over the past approx. 2 to 3 years. It remains one of the most attractive growth markets in global pharma. According to Grand View Research, the obesity segment alone, of the GLP-1 market, is expected to grow at a CAGR of approx. 18.5% between 2025 and 2030.

There is, however, a wide dispersion in estimates of the long-term market size. Depending on the source, estimates range from approx. USD 80 billion to USD 185 billion by the early 2030s. The direction is, in my opinion, clear, but this wide dispersion is a sign to me that in some cases expectations may be too optimistic with regard to current valuations.

Over the past two to three years, the market has been dominated by a simple dynamic: demand significantly exceeded supply. This led to strong volume growth and the emergence of a new phenomenon: the compounding market. Patients were willing to pay out-of-pocket mainly because of the limited availability of branded products and high list prices from Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly.

In my opinion, what we are now seeing is a transition into a normalized phase. Volumes continue to grow at a strong pace (at least in Eli Lilly's case), but pricing has started to come down. This partly due to more intense competition between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly but also due to ongoing pressure from compounding pharmacies. I think going forward, pricing, access (like insurance coverage), and go-to-market execution will be key for success.

Compounders

The rise of compounding pharmacies is often described as a temporary regulatory loophole, which is broadly true. At the same time, the impact has been meaningful, as they gained traction mainly through lower prices, availability during shortages, and easy access via telehealth. This primarily affected the cash-pay segment but still distorted the overall market and put pressure on branded products. Lilly has been less affected, not because of chemistry, but mainly due to faster supply normalization and more active enforcement against compounding pharmacies, supported by stronger commercial execution. As supply constraints ease and regulation tightens, the impact should fade, but the episode highlights that Novo's franchise is less defensible than previously assumed once supply normalizes.

Novo Nordisk: Cheap for a Reason

Novo Nordisk is a very focused company with regard to therapeutic area. Diabetes remains, with 66% (GLP-1 and insulin) the largest revenue source; however, obesity is significantly outgrowing this segment and has become the key driver of incremental growth.

From a growth perspective, FY25 further highlighted this dynamic. Obesity continues to grow at a significantly higher rate than diabetes GLP-1, showing that the investment case is largely tied to obesity.

While this focus is well understood at the segment level, the concentration becomes more visible when looking at the active ingredient. Ozempic (diabetes) and Wegovy (obesity), the two main revenue drivers, are both based on the same molecule, semaglutide. The same holds for the oral version currently being rolled out. As a result, approx. two-thirds of group revenue is linked to a single molecule.

So, Novo's obesity and diabetes franchise is built around semaglutide. While the products target different patient populations, they share the same underlying mechanism.

This creates a large vulnerability. Any pressure on semaglutide impacts the entire growth engine at the same time. This is not just a theoretical concern but something that is already visible in recent developments. For FY26, management expects a revenue decline of approx. 5-13%. This shows the downside of having a highly concentrated revenue profile.

Pipeline

Novo's pipeline is often viewed as being weak. In my view, this is somewhat too negative. The company is active across most relevant areas, like next-generation obesity treatments, cardiovascular indications, and other metabolic diseases.

The key issue is less the breadth of the pipeline and more the timing. CagriSema shows generally good outcomes but is not clearly superior to Lilly's existing offering and will enter the market with a significant delay. Other programs are relevant, but not the main growth driver.

In my view, there is currently nothing in the pipeline that really addresses the core issue. In pharma, this matters a lot. You cannot just replace a dominant asset overnight. This takes years, not quarters.

Given the increasing competition and limited near-term pipeline impact, I don't see a clear catalyst for a quick re-rating. Novo will likely recover over time. But this process is more likely going to happen in years and not in quarters.

We have seen similar situations before in pharma. There are many examples, one of which is AstraZeneca (AZN), where the company went through several years of pressure after losing key products like Crestor, which also impacted FCF.

I use FCF here as a proxy to show how long it can take until a pipeline starts to compensate for these losses. I don't think that Novo is completely impaired, but the recovery is also unlikely to be quick.

Eli Lilly: Strong Execution - But Fully Priced

Eli Lilly is currently the clear operational leader in the GLP-1 market. The company has executed exceptionally well in getting over supply issues and driving physician adoption.

From a growth perspective, Lilly clearly outperformed in FY25. Tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) gained strong traction. In my view, this is mainly driven by better efficacy and also by stronger execution and a better ramp-up of supply. This translated into very strong volume growth, which is also visible in the chart above, with Lilly gaining share to approx. 60% of prescriptions.

Although Eli Lilly is also heavily dependent on a single molecule, tirzepatide, the dependency is lower at approx. 56% compared to approx. 67% at Novo Nordisk. In addition, Lilly's other franchises, like Oncology, continue to grow, meaning the company is less concentrated overall. Approx. 74% of revenue is linked to diabetes and obesity, compared to approx. 94% at Novo. This makes Lilly more diversified.

Another important difference is the next generation of products. Lilly's upcoming oral obesity drug is based on a new molecule, orforglipron, rather than the same active ingredient. This reduces the risk that potential setbacks affect the entire franchise at once.

This does not mean Lilly is immune to the dynamics discussed earlier. Pricing pressure is starting to emerge as supply improves and competition increases. However, Lilly has so far been less affected, as pricing pressure has been offset by strong volume growth.

What makes me more cautious on Lilly is the valuation. At around 21x EV/EBITDA (NTM), a lot of the good news is already in the price. If volume growth slows or pricing comes under more pressure, there is not much room for disappointment.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, the difference between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly is significant.

The wider pharma peer group trades at approx. low- to mid-teens on an EV/EBITDA basis. Higher-quality names such as Novartis (NVS), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), or AstraZeneca typically trade towards the upper end of that range.

Eli Lilly is a clear outlier, trading at a premium of approx. 21-27x EV/EBITDA. This reflects strong growth and very good execution. Novo Nordisk, on the other hand, trades at a discount at approx. 7-9x.

At first glance, Novo might look more attractive given the lower valuation. In my view, however, the discount is largely justified by the dependence on semaglutide and the lack of a near-term catalyst. Lilly, on the other hand, trades at a premium where a lot is already priced in.

The market is effectively pricing Novo for uncertainty and Lilly for near-perfect execution. In my opinion, both valuations broadly reflect the underlying fundamentals.

Risks

There are some factors that could significantly alter my investment thesis.

First, the GLP-1 market could grow faster than currently expected, and pricing pressure may remain more limited than what I assumed. Especially if insurance coverage expands further. In this case, both Novo and Lilly could continue to deliver strong growth, making current valuations look more reasonable.

Second, my view assumes that Novo's pipeline will not deliver a near-term catalyst. If CagriSema or other assets show clearly superior outcomes or come to market faster than expected, this could materially improve Novo's competitive position and lead to a re-rating.

Third, Lilly has executed very well so far. If the company continues to gain market share, scale supply efficiently, and offset pricing pressure with volume growth, the current valuation may be justified for longer than what I expected.

Conclusion

Both Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly remain good companies with strong long-term positions in one of the most attractive markets in pharma.

However, from current levels, the setup looks less attractive. Novo faces a period of structural adjustment and lacks a clear near-term catalyst. Lilly continues to execute very well but is already priced for it.

In my view, the market is currently treating Novo as broken and Lilly as unstoppable. In reality, I think both narratives are likely too extreme.

As the GLP-1 market normalizes, the setup for both companies looks less attractive. From today's levels, neither stock stands out as particularly attractive, and I would wait for a better entry point.
